author: @IndividualPerspective
text: There's a good Cristian principle that if you see it you become somewhat responsible for it..

author: @sc-lj9cp
text: I notice you often have a bit of a dig about the education system and it makes me think of an educationist called Steiner also advocated for a hands on type of education, where children learn through practical experience and their own investigations, obviously with a teachers guidance.In the same vein I’ve also heard of summer camps in the USA where they take apart things like washing machines and rebuild them. It claims to encourage creativity and I certainly go along with this myself. My own children went to a Steiner school and it certainly gave them an ability to seek out answers without being told, plus an appreciation of the world and its seasons that exists around them. Very much enjoyed your talk.

author: @totherarf
text:
Well that was interesting!

One point I will make about genes though is that they Do have a large effect on us, but not in the way you might expect. If you look at the high intelligence (how we judge that is another issue) and when they marry and have kids with their peers you find that the kids are dummies ..... all relative of course ...... BUT if those kids have kids with other kids of high IQ people Their kids (the grandkids of the high IQ people) They are above average in IQ!

OK I will take the dog for a walk and ponder on your words for a bit ..... I often do my best thinking walking the dog! ;o)

author: @wrekced
text:
Robert, I remember some studies on genes and intelligence from about ten years ago that showed that the factors that make a person intelligent are only about 50% heritable. They showed that even with good genes, intelligence is highly dependant on the enviornment a person grows up in. I think you are pretty close in your position to what I see in the scienctific literature I have read on the subject.

About your intro talk: If a tree falls in the forest and nobody is around; does anyone give a sh...?

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: My bad, it is termed "digital physics" yet I call it digital metaphysics due to an unmanifest aspect reality, where chaos , infinity an disorder must somehow have correlation in the ordering of the universe from a chaotic magical void that suffers lapses (id est ordering) in being so chaotic that order forms in this latent cosmic stew of "nothingness"...  https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_physics  My previous comment suggests the evolution of order into the manifst physical universe based on the potential of unmanifest (nothingness). This also refers to some Daoist notion of "The Dao that an be spoken is not the true Tao, or, The name is not the thing named. From a semiotics perspective this is a pretty simple notion, we know that the written word is only a referrant referring to something. For instance 0 has an infinite numerical potential (its increase is infinite) while 1 has an (infinity-1) potential to increase, as it is already one. However i think numbers are also qualitative and these properties need to somehow be incorporate in a quantitative model of mathemetics, and which is why I believe the Universe "devolved" from an infinite chaotic dynamic state to this ordered state. Anyways... ~ The digital physics article is a good read, I'm just grasping at straws :)

author: @joejoejoejoejoejoe4391
text: I think that power stations are about 33% efficient, the power distribution grid is incredibly efficient for what it does, about 90%, so not to bad as a whole, more efficient than home generation, unless you use waste heat.

author: @joejoejoejoejoejoe4391
text:
I think it's at best a bit "messy" - surely an already intelligent person will seek out mental stimulation, and even in an unstimulating environment, they will find something to study and think about. Perhaps it's a snowball effect, once a baby is stimulated, it will become more intelligent, and therefore seek out more stimulation. 
It seems odd to me that the most profound inventors/scientists of the past (Hero, De Vinci, Newton, Tesla, Einstein) seem to pop up at random, if it was anything to do with genetics, you'd expect them to come from a long line of highly intelligent ancestors.

author: @a3b36a04
text: A quest for immortality? Youtube sometimes delete,  "compress" and unlist older videos without warning.

author: @wrench71
text: 27KWHR! In the time it took to watch this video, the fuel truck in front of me pumped 19500 gallons of fuel into a 747. Hows that for two ends of a spectrum!

author: @gunsarrus7836
text: I disagree on all being equally intelligent, genes define the patterns that build the neural networks and those set the caps on intelligence. While one has to work the brain to maximize intelligence without the underlying neural network required working it will only get you so far. This is why some genetic populations are very low IQ and others are higher but also within those populations there is a lot of variation.

author: @crawkn
text: Those are a lot of good analogies, and they are useful for understanding abstract or complex things like intelligence and the nature / nurture interplay. There are some who may be prone to confusing the analog for the thing, so it's important to attend to where the analogy breaks down. It's a great suggestion that people do their own experimentation, but one reason people learn from others in videos is that they have inadequate resources, time being one, to do everything that interests them themselves.

author: @alexscheitel7668
text: These types of videos are great info and mind opening!

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: Science may be constrained by its very outlook, rather than readjusting its outlook or even the fundamentals of math. For example what *if* something was so fast, it could already be "everywhere" at the same time, i.e. it is SO fast, that it is already everywhere else, at the same time, and in a sense is SO fast it could be said to not be moving, but only be an infinitely dynamic state, alraedy being everywhere. This numerable(?) potential could be called the "omnipotence of god / or creation". Here and there become relative notions. Somehow this is my personal take on the theories of "digital metaphysics" (which I know little of but are quite extensive - I never delve too deeply for some reason but quickly form my own ideas on what is already said, and then will deviate into my own theories). And I'd say that the cosmos as we know it is a reduction of this super-dynamic state of potential of the underlying fabric of reality that contradicts itself, and these contradictions cause physical manifest reality from the non-manifest metaphysical realm of potential. As Parmenides would say, there is only existence. My take on his ideas are/is that, because, if non-existence exists, then it is not non-existence. If non-existence does not exist, then there is only existence (the infinite zero-nyx of infinite chaotic potential, everything reduced to the ordered 1 of the prime material / physical  world through conflicting states of potential - to say the Universe is not an irrational system, even if it is "chaotic and infinite" with infinite potential. So I'd say an unbearable charge of potential so fast (so fast and infinite in its ways and places, being everywhere at the same time an infinite amount of times immediately all at the same time) and absolutely and infinite it's already everywhere at the same time in an infinite number of ways. But somehow these ways contradicted each other and we have the reductionist laws of physics to content with from this old Norse God's Void or Greek Chaos / Nyx, which is boundless and beyond laws (I say in my own words that order is chaos overlapping itself and being redundant. We think it is order but actually it is just the redundancy of chaos. This creates conflict in the system of latent potentials to form finite realities. Much like redundantly metaphysical potential redoubled, becoming manifestly physical, much like the notion of quantum entanglement, or that the potential for something to exist happens more than once, and in its redundancy becomes manifest and physical, as a literal manifest example of my theory (I do not know, it's just a science idea that might reflect some veracity to what I am saying beyond my ability to prove it which i don't think is very accurate, i.e. quantum entanglement as the redundancy of chaos, creating order and physical reality)... I quote, "In Norse mythology, Ginnungagap is the primordial, magical void mentioned in three poems from the Poetic Edda and the Gylfaginning." Sorry for my rant. I really should get back to reality and paint, but my father is so rude and unkind to me that I am constantly taking sleeping pills so that I can remain unconscious in the world of abstraction, and cannot get to planning my paintings, which I really loved to do, he is so patently rude that unconsciousness is my only respite...I also believe dark energy and gravity are two opposites (dark energy is the accruement of a lack of, or slow reduction of gravity, i.e. the universe's potential being recharged for another "Big Bang" (I should call it rather a Big Tear as it will snap open again like a huge firecracker once not a single scintilla remains. Another Scintillion - a neologism of mine -  (will form), but its state is unsustainable) for some reason, unless it can be rebranded as being merely a transitional phase, in which its "instability" is only the nature of its being a flux period in a larger-phased process. The Big Bang then is not the causal source of the cosmos but the effect of the underlying fabric thereof. I'm not talking split infinitives here, I don't even know why subatomic particles have such difficult names from a relational standpoint to each other so as not to simplify their relational roles,  that they are hard to remember for those of us with poor memory... as all the gravity of reductionist contradictions (that form the elements of the periodic table, ultimately, forms of matter in it crudest sense, and their properties and fields) of physical realities and all their energies are spent, only then will the Universe be capable of another Big Bang. I think there was an infinite number of infinitesimal bangs everywhere, that tore the universe apart quite violently, their joint effort, escalating from the subatomic scale to the cosmological scale, could be called the Big Bang. It's just my opinion and I may of course be totally wrong. A very interesting talk, my apologies to ramble. I don't make much sense quite possibly because my reference framework uses certain terms in their own way not related to the set nomenclature of the canon of the empirical sciences as I didn't finish highschool, so my apologies. I do try my best though to create some syncretic ideation to reconcile the world of physics with the metaphysical world. I did study linguistics (semiotics) but sadly they refused my bursary even though I am entitled to do my honours and masters, and just lie around thinking and avoiding knowledge constructs as best as I can, except specific ones I am however financially incapable of putting into practice. Love the show!

author: @TheBaconWizard
text:
There's quite a lot of evidence that the "us" that we thing of as being the self, should be thought-of as being more like a culture than an individual both in terms of cerebral intelligence but also with many influences that one would normally think-of as being purely chemical; the microbiome in your gut for example changing gene expression, changing hormones or neurotransmitters all of which can have a profound and very real effect on how you view the world in any given moment, just as taking a psychoactive drug would do. 
We build a mental model of the world that includes the self, which is informed by incoming information connected by a narrative that we have created. The narrative we create says that "I" made that decision and here are the reasons why. It isn't actually so. 
What we experience as concious thought, is an emergent property of lots of other intelligent processes working sometimes together or sometimes at-odds with each other, much like a human culture. 
I mention it, because considering ourselves to be part of a greater whole intelligence, seems to me, a lot easier when you know that you are already not a single entity at-all, but the sum of many parts each of which could be considered intelligent and over which you have limited control.

author: @Ken00001010
text: Asking "What is it like to be a cat?" links back to the famous Thomas Nagel paper "What is it like to be a bat?" wich is, perhaps, a bigger reach. In thinking about these questions I focus on the word "like." Asking with "like" is asking for analogy. Perhaps we can imagine having a strong urge to chase mice, or clean our hands by licking them, because those are within the scope of analogy. However, the deeper question is not just what it is "like" but rather what it "is." That step is from analogy or representation to being. We may think of ways it is something to be like a cat, but we can't get to what it *is* to actually be one, and the same is also true for being any other person, as well.

author: @emanonn
text:
I have heard before that genetic expression is a bit like those little art cards with the scratch off surface. Genes create the whole color splash behind the scratch off material, and the environment abrades the material. While the environment doesn't create new genes or make expressions that aren't already encoded in the genes, it does affect which genes are expressed and how. 

It's a bit of a weird analogy, but it really set the dynamic nature of the whole thing straight for me!

replies.author: @Ken00001010
replies.text: Folks should be careful about using the word "control" re either genes or their environment. It's really a co-operative interaction. This comes from how Evolution selected the mechanisms of living things. You can ask the question, "Which came first: the genes or their environment?" and that is, of course, answered by "neither." The genes and the mechanisms that express them (their environment) coevolved, and neither is "controlling" anything, just together developing into something.

replies.author: @frankbenevolence
replies.text: How or where life got started is hard to know, the environment definitely existed before life but you raise a good point.  My understanding life is an adaptive, self-replicating feedback loop that arose in response to, and is fuelled by the environment, like a fire that started billions of years ago and refuses to go out.

author: @TheBaconWizard
text: If anyone is interested in learning more about this subject (genes, environment, intelligence and behaviour) I HIGHLY recommend the work of Dr Robert Sapolsky, there are lectures on youtube and in podcasts that a free or he has many groundbreaking books.

replies.author: @Ken00001010
replies.text: I recommend Sapolsky's latest book on Free Will.
